Name: distortion_optimized_quantization
Title: Theoretical Foundations of Distortion-Optimized Quantization for Neural Network Compression
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that leveraging a robust rate-distortion framework can yield a novel quantization strategy that minimizes task-relevant distortion more effectively than existing heuristics, providing a clear operational meaning to quantization decisions.
Related Work: Existing literature has focused on heuristic-based quantization methods with limited theoretical grounding, such as AMP-ViT and SNIP. Our proposal distinguishes itself by integrating a rigorous rate-distortion approach to derive quantization strategies that are not only theoretically sound but also empirically validated, addressing the gap in understanding the trade-offs between model capacity and quantization-induced distortions.
Abstract: In the quest for efficient neural network deployments, quantization serves as a critical tool for reducing model size and computational demands. However, current quantization techniques often rely on heuristics that lack a solid theoretical foundation regarding their impact on task performance. This proposal introduces a novel approach to quantization grounded in rate-distortion theory, aiming to minimize task-relevant distortion while adhering to specific bit-rate constraints. We will derive a quantization framework that explicitly quantifies the relationship between model compression and predictive accuracy, leveraging mutual information and Fisher information metrics. By establishing achievability and converse bounds on distortion rates, our method will provide a clear operational meaning to quantization decisions, ultimately leading to a more principled approach to model compression. We aim to demonstrate the effectiveness of our framework through a series of experiments on various neural architectures, comparing our proposed method against state-of-the-art quantization techniques.
Experiments: 1. Derive a quantization scheme based on the rate-distortion framework for a simple neural architecture (e.g., a small convolutional network) and evaluate its performance against existing quantization methods on standard datasets.

2. Investigate the relationship between bit allocation and task performance by systematically varying the bit-widths of individual layers and observing the resulting distortions using metrics such as accuracy loss and model size.
3. Conduct experiments on larger models (e.g., ResNet-50) to validate the scalability of the proposed quantization method and assess how well it maintains accuracy compared to the theoretical bounds.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The theoretical framework may not translate directly to practical implementations, necessitating further empirical validation.
The complexity of deriving optimal quantization parameters may lead to challenges in computation time and resource allocation during experiments.
Potential limitations in the generalization of results across different model architectures and datasets.
